posts.name: Noah C @ P U R 5 0
posts.cooked:
Pours a medium gold. Focused aromas of lemon curd, key limes, sea spray, some underripe pear, as well as an undercurrent of complex aromas of age that I’m having some difficulty parsing out. I can only explain it by saying that young Chablis always smells very bright, and this is bright as well, but with a few streaks of dark thrown in there as well.

Acidity is high and the wine feels very focused, just starting to soften out around the edges a bit. There are strong flavors of baked citrus desserts while still remaining completely dry. There is a powdery chalky quality that I associate with Chablis on the finish.

Wow- this is really good. It will last a few more years at least, but my recommendation is to drink it now. Great stuff.

posts.name: Jordan_Rock
posts.cooked: I’ve been getting the Côte de Léchet the last few vintages and have loved them. I haven’t opened my 2007 yet, but I’m looking forward to it.

posts.name: Dennis Atick
posts.cooked: A wine from the future!

posts.name: suzanne_camhi
posts.cooked: I believe this was a recent release from Defaix

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
It will last a few more years at least, but my recommendation is to drink it now.

Sounds pretty worrisome, as Defaix wines are known to age wonderfully and to my understanding this is the most recent release of this label - it sounds pretty weird if Defaix first ages their wines for 15 years and when the wines are released, they’d last only a few more years.

posts.name: S_Yæt€s
posts.cooked: Just got emails here in the states about the '07s.

posts.name: Noah C @ P U R 5 0
posts.cooked:
It will last a few more years at least, but my recommendation is to drink it now.

Sounds pretty worrisome, as Defaix wines are known to age wonderfully and to my understanding this is the most recent release of this label - it sounds pretty weird if Defaix first ages their wines for 15 years and when the wines are released, they’d last only a few more years.

You know, Otto, you have a good point here. First of all, I’m not a pro, so everyone should take my aging predictions with a grain of salt.

But, it’s relevant to mention that I had a 2004 version of this wine not too long ago that was much fresher and would probably age far longer. Not sure if the difference can be chalked up to differences in the 04 vs 07 vintages, or simple bottle variation. Those more experienced than me might be able to comment on the bottle to bottle consistency with this producer. For what it’s worth, both wines were under natural cork.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: I would say that these wines, given their very long élevage on the lees in steel, taste mature in the best sense when release, but then age, generally, very slowly. Defaix’s 2006s are much more youthful than many 2006 Chablis today, for example.

posts.name: Jordan_Rock
posts.cooked: Personally, I don’t get what’s worrisome about a wine that’s released with 14-15 years of bottle age when the producer thinks it’s ready to go and elicits a “Wow- this is really good” reaction, regardless of whether the wine will continue to improve for many more years.

posts.name: Ɠřәǥǥ Ɠřәәŋþąųӎ
posts.cooked:
Personally, I don’t get what’s worrisome about a wine that’s released with 14-15 years of bottle age when the producer thinks it’s ready to go and elicits a “Wow- this is really good” reaction, regardless of whether the wine will continue to improve for many more years.

Taking Defaix’s reputation into consideration, a recent release should not be sloping downward and one would expect, based on their elevage, a considerably longer life than a few years (of course realizing this is one person’s opinion). I have some of the '07 Lys on the way, so I have reason for concern. As usual, YMMV.

Taking Defaix’s reputation into consideration, a recent release should not be sloping downward and one would expect, based on their elevage, a considerably longer life than a few years (of course realizing this is one person’s opinion). I have some of the '07 Lys on the way, so I have reason for concern. As usual, YMMV.

I learned long ago, and continue to believe, that a wine stays on a plateau about as long as it took to reach that plateau . . . so I expect good things from these reasonably priced Chablis for a long time.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Just tasted one of the 2007s (Vaillon) at the domaine. Very youthful and vibrant, but with great flesh, after over a decade of maturation in tank! Sadly since my visit last year, when we tasted 2006s, Defaix has blown through 2007 and 2008, so we are now onto the 2009s. Based on how good this 2007 is, I would be inclined to stock up if I had access.

posts.name: Martin Steinley
posts.cooked:
Very youthful and vibrant, but with great flesh, after over a decade of maturation in tank!

In anticipation of the 2007s arriving in my warehouse a couple of months ago, I tasted through the 2006s with some Burgundy-savvy friends and the universal opinion was just this. There is no hurry to drink the Defaix crus following release; rather, simply enjoy the gift the domaine offers.
Had a bottle this past weekend and was impressed. Fantastic value. Will happily continue to seek out these releases.

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked: bought 08 also—then heard some were oxidized—my first b was not. Recently saw an offer for 10. They must be cleaning out the cave.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: No, 2009 has now been bottled and is coming onto the market. Next up for bottling is 2010.

posts.name: Dav1d S@wyer
posts.cooked: Drank through four bottles of '06 1ers (Vaillons, Les Lys, Cote de Lechet) and found all of them to be nice wines but fairly monolithic. They all lacked complexity. Maybe they were still young?
